preamble: The transformation from the voiced alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʒ/ to the voiced palatal approximant /j/ is a common phonetic shift, primarily characterized by lenition. This process involves the weakening of the affricate, where the complete closure characteristic of an affricate is reduced to a partial closure, resulting in an approximant. This deaffrication is a natural sound change observed across various languages and dialects, often driven by articulatory ease.
phoneticEffects: Lenition, Deaffrication, Spirantization (in a broader sense of weakening)

examples.note: A synchronic phonological process of lenition.

sources: Johnstone, Thomas M. (1967). The Sound Change “j > y” in the Arabic Dialects of Peninsular Arabia. Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies 28 (2): 233–241.
